reviewNotes: Primary transmission and supporting relations are kept separate; the U-M raw package contains the XML encoding and TIFF facsimile as parallel members.
Relations 001-009 are exhaustively represented in four explicit primary paths; every other relation appears exactly once in a supporting group.

The same 38,600,162-byte U-M raw ZIP is attached to its package, encoding, and TIFF-facsimile access routes, producing 54 local-copy occurrences from 52 unique paths while counting its stored bytes only once.
Wülfing's 1901 article expression, the later Johnson Reprint Company journal carrier, and the 2024 Internet Archive facsimile are modeled as separate layers.
The current EETS and Routledge products are catalog descriptions of mechanical reprints, not newly edited scholarly editions.
No complete public facsimile of MS. Laud Misc. 595 was found as of 2026-07-17; the cached Bodleian IIIF object is complete only for its two-image provider selection.

The manuscript, its modern images and delivery files, the medieval abstract works, scholarly editions, carriers, catalog records, and repository files have separate component- and access-scoped rights statements.

lineage.summary: The pinned repository XML represents U-M CME item APE9595.0001.001, an OCR-derived encoding of J. Ernst Wülfing's 1902-1903 EETS edition of the complete 18,664-line Laud Troy Book. Wülfing transcribed the unique witness, Bodleian MS. Laud Misc. 595. The abstract Middle English work directly adapts Guido delle Colonne's Historia destructionis Troiae; possible direct use of Benoît's Roman de Troie remains tentative. The graph distinguishes repository artifact, raw package, digital encoding, edition facsimiles and carriers, scholarly edition, abstract works, manuscript witness, catalog records, and modern reprint descriptions. Fifty-two fixity-verified source-cache captures are linked to their exact access routes; six derived audits remain evidence-only.

assertion.notes: Retain the verbal source attribution without inventing numeric bounds. Do not substitute the manuscript catalog's 1400-1410 production date for the work-composition date.

extent.description: One complete verse work represented by one BODY DIV1 book, 667 encoded LG groups, and 18,664 L elements. The 667 groups are encoding/navigation units, many lacking a stanza type, and are not asserted as 667 literary stanzas or separate content items.

formStatements.description: A complete 18,664-line Middle English poem in rhyming four-stress couplets, described by DMER as aa4 bb4 and so on.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin rubrics and the Latin/French source works do not change the primary language of the Middle English work.
Describe the medieval poem, not Wülfing's two-volume publication or the U-M digital item.
Count one work once and use one count-bearing content part.
Use sections=1 for the single BODY work division and verse_groups=667 for the encoding groups; do not call all 667 groups literary stanzas.
Keep the witness's 1400-1410 production date separate from the work's early-fifteenth-century composition date.
Keep the work's tentative dialect region separate from the manuscript's broad English origin.
Keep source-work authors Guido and Benoît out of the target-work author responsibility.
Do not normalize the early-fifteenth-century composition statement to 1400-1425; no inspected source supplies those exact bounds.
The poem is not authored by Wülfing, Guido delle Colonne, Benoît de Sainte-Maure, or the Bodleian Library.
The 667 LG elements are not proved to be 667 authorial stanzas.
The publication split at lines 10,876/10,877 is not a two-work boundary.
The manuscript's 1400-1410 date is not automatically the work's exact composition date.
A surviving physical Latin or French exemplar used by the English poet has not been identified.
Direct use of Benoît is not confirmed.

next_steps: Preserve both as source-attributed tentative statements; do not resolve by preference.
openQuestions.question: How should current dialect scholarship reconcile DMER's East Midlands? localization with Wülfing's older North-West Midlands hypothesis?

next_steps: Keep the metadata relation related_to and the lineage relation tentative at medium confidence, grounded in Wülfing and ARLIMA rather than DMER.
openQuestions.question: Did the Laud poet consult Benoît's Roman de Troie directly, or are the parallels mediated through Guido?

assertion.notes: Preserve as a source-attributed historical hypothesis rather than reconciling it silently with current DMER.

No medieval author is named in the encoded work or Wülfing's source study. Catalog the poet as anonymous rather than assigning the editor, source-work authors, or holding library; DMER is not used as evidence for this responsibility.

summaries.value: An anonymous early-fifteenth-century Middle English Troy romance in 18,664 rhyming four-stress couplet lines, translating and adapting Guido delle Colonne's Latin Historia destructionis Troiae and possibly drawing directly on Benoît de Sainte-Maure's Roman de Troie. It narrates the origins, siege, and destruction of Troy and survives uniquely in Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 595.

The record covers only the complete medieval English poem. Wülfing's two publication parts, edition apparatus, print carriers, page facsimiles, catalog records, XML/TXT encodings, and archive package are lineage layers rather than additional work parts.
